Title: Milolo jumps 20pct on Leap Market debut
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Home-grown designer toy marker Milolo Bhd debuted on the Leap Market of Bursa Malaysia today at 12 sen, marking a 20 per cent premium over its initial public offering (IPO) price of 10 sen.
MORE TO COME

Title: Newark landing scare as United Airlines plane hits truck and lamppost
Article:
NEW YORK: A United Airlines plane hit a lamppost and a delivery truck on a New Jersey highway while landing Sunday at Newark Liberty International Airport, officials told AFP.
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) officials said the Boeing 767 landed safely after arriving from Venice, Italy, and the agency would investigate the incident.
No injuries were reported on the plane, and the truck driver was taken to the hospital with minor injuries and released, the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey said in a statement
The jet was carrying 221 passengers and 10 crew members.
"While on approach to land at Newark Liberty International Airport, United Airlines Flight 169 struck a light pole on the New Jersey Turnpike around 2 p.m. local time on Sunday, May 3," the FAA said in a statement, adding that the plane sustained minor damage.
The Port Authority said in a statement that the plane caused "damage to a light post and a tractor trailer traveling south on the NJ Turnpike."
The H&S Bakery truck "was unscathed" but "the wheel of the commercial plane crashed into the driver's window," according to Chuck Paterakis, a senior vice president for the company, who added "the truck driver suffered small cuts from the incident."
United Airlines said in a statement it plans to "conduct a rigorous flight safety investigation into the incident and our crew has been removed from service as part of this process."
New Jersey State Police, who responded to the highway incident, and aircraft manufacturer Boeing did not immediately respond to AFP's request for comment.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against US dollar ahead of OPR announcement
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened higher against the US dollar on the first trading day of May, with the upcoming Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting later this week, developments in the United States–Iran conflict and the release of the US non-farm payroll (NFP) data expected to set the tone for the market.
At 8 am, the domestic unit gained against the greenback to 3.9590/9725 from 3.9690/9740 at last Thursday's close.
The local market was closed on Friday in conjunction with the Labour Day public holiday.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the BNM's MPC members will decide the overnight policy rate (OPR) on May 7.
"Most economists expect the OPR to be unchanged and the latest assessment on the Iran war sees no signs of ebbing while crude oil prices remain elevated," he added.
"There is also the NFP data points in the US on Friday which is quite critical for US Federal Reserves decision on rates. In that sense, we expect to ringgit to maintain its narrow range posture as market sentiments will remain cautious," he told Bernama.
Mohd Afzanizam expects US dollar-ringgit to range between RM3.95 and RM3.97 today.
Meanwhile, the ringgit traded mostly lower against a basket of major currencies.
It eased against the Japanese yen to 2.5226/5315 from 2.4907/4942 at the close on Thursday, traded almost flat against the euro at 4.6415/6574 from 4.6417/6476 and slipped versus the British pound to 5.3759/3943 from 5.3593/3661 previously.
The local currency was mixed against regional peers.
It depreciated against the Singapore dollar to 3.1095/1206 from 3.1061/1103 at Thursday's close, fell against the Thai baht to 12.1812/2306 from 12.1711/1932 yesterday, but was marginally higher against the Indonesian rupiah at 228.3/229.2 from 228.7/229.2 and the Philippine peso at 6.44/4/6.47 from 6.45/6.47 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Court rules Bursa Malaysia justified in dismissing senior executive for misusing system access
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Industrial Court ruled that Bursa Malaysia Bhd was justified in dismissing a senior executive with 35 years of service for misusing his system access to obtain and share his colleagues' private messages.
Industrial Court chairman Ahmad Razif Mohd Sidek said T.A. Robert was dismissed with just cause after committing serious misconduct amounting to a gross breach of trust.
Robert started his career as a data entry operator on Feb 1, 1991, before being promoted to senior executive of access control on July 1, 2019.
The court heard that his role gave him access to employees' emails and Skype conversations, including those of the chief executive officer, but only upon specific request and prior approval.
However, evidence showed Robert had accessed and extracted private conversations from the system without authorisation and used selected excerpts for personal purposes, including sharing them with an external party via his personal email.
"The claimant's deliberate disclosure of private conversations to an unauthorised external party constitutes a gross breach of trust.
"Such conduct was a clear misuse of his privileged position and incompatible with the responsibilities entrusted to him," Razif said in his grounds of judgment recently.
The court also found that the claimant had engaged in inappropriate conduct towards a female colleague, including attempting to track her location using a mobile application and sending emails containing vulgar and threatening remarks.
His actions, the court said, caused fear, distress and embarrassment to the colleague.
The court said all charges levelled against him in a show-cause letter had been proven on a balance of probabilities, based on his own admissions during the company's internal investigation, his written explanation and his testimony in court.
Robert had argued that his dismissal was harsh and unjust, citing his long and previously unblemished service.
He also contended that no proper domestic inquiry had been conducted.
However, the court held that the absence of a formal domestic inquiry was not fatal, as long as the employer had carried out a fair investigation and allowed the employee to be heard.
The court found that Bursa Malaysia had conducted a thorough inquiry, interviewed relevant witnesses, gathered documentary evidence and issued a show-cause letter, to which the claimant had responded.
"The misconducts committed were grave and went to the very foundation of the employment relationship," the court said.
The court said the claimant, as an experienced IT personnel, was expected to uphold the highest standards of integrity in safeguarding confidential information, but instead had misused his access for personal ends.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: GameStop makes unsolicited offer to buy eBay for about US$56bil
Article:
NEW YORK: GameStop has made an unsolicited offer to acquire US online marketplace eBay Inc for about US$56 billion, the Wall Street Journal reported on Sunday, citing an interview with GameStop chief executive officer Ryan Cohen.
GameStop has built a roughly five per cent stake in eBay and is offering US$125 a share in cash and stock, Cohen told the WSJ. The offer represents a premium of about 20 per cent to eBay's last closing price on Friday.
If eBay is not receptive, Cohen said he was prepared to run a proxy fight and take the offer directly to shareholders, the report added.
"EBay should be worth - and will be worth - a lot more money," Cohen said in the WSJ interview. "I'm thinking about turning eBay into something worth hundreds of billions of dollars."
The chief executive officer of the videogame retailer said he has secured a commitment letter from TD Bank for about US$20 billion in debt financing.
GameStop could seek support from external investors, including Middle Eastern sovereign-wealth funds, to help fund the deal, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter.
GameStop holds a market value of nearly US$12 billion, while eBay has a market capitalisation of about US$46 billion, according to Reuters calculations.
TD Bank, GameStop and eBay did not immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.
A potential deal would upend the usual M&A playbook. It is rare for a public company to target one nearly four times its size; such deals typically rely on heavy debt, stock issuance, or both - banking on future earnings of the combined company to justify the cost.
Grapevine, Texas-based GameStop is expected to disclose details of its offer later on Sunday, the WSJ said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK set to enter talks to join the EU's US$105.9bil Ukraine loan
Article:
LONDON: Britain is set to enter talks to join the European Union's 78 billion pound loan (US$106 billion) to Ukraine, the government said on Sunday, in a further sign of deepening European defence ties under rising US pressure.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer is expected on Monday to tell a summit in Armenia's capital Yerevan of the European Political Community - a discussion forum set up after Russia's invasion in 2022 - that Britain wants to work with the EU to support Ukraine in getting vital military equipment, his office said.
The loan, approved by the EU last month, is set to cover two-thirds of Ukraine's needs for the next two years, with the bulk of that amount earmarked for military spending as Kyiv defends itself against Russia's four-year war.
The extra funding could also unlock opportunities for British businesses to meet Ukraine's urgent needs, particularly in the defence sector, the government said in a statement.
FURTHER SANCTIONS ON RUSSIAN COMPANIES
Britain, which has imposed wide-ranging sanctions on Russia since the war began in 2022, will also announce another tranche of "stinging sanctions" on Russian companies this week to disrupt military supply chains, it said.
Starmer's visit, the first by a British leader to Armenia since former Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher in 1990, comes as the Trump administration pushes Europe to take more responsibility for the continent's defence.
European countries, including Germany, France and Britain, have recently come under further pressure from Washington after refusing to join the US and Israel's war on Iran.
"When the UK and the European Union work together, we all reap the benefits — and in these volatile times we need to go further and faster on defence to keep people safe," Starmer said in the statement.
He has previously called for stronger defence integration within the continent to cut NATO's over-reliance on the US, hinting at further alignment with the EU's single market and deeper economic integration, six years after Brexit.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil slips after Trump says US will help free ships stranded in Strait of Hormuz
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil prices eased on Monday after US President Donald Trump said the United States would begin an effort to free up ships stranded in the Strait of Hormuz, however the lack of a US-Iran peace deal kept prices supported above US$100.
Brent crude futures fell 64 cents, or 0.59 per cent, to US$107.53 a barrel by 2308 GMT after settling down US$2.23 on Friday.
US West Texas Intermediate was at US$101.10 a barrel, down 84 cents, or 0.82 per cent, following a US$3.13 loss on Friday.
"For the good of Iran, the Middle East, and the United States, we have told these countries that we will guide their ships safely out of these restricted waterways, so that they can freely and ably get on with their business," Trump wrote in a post on his Truth Social site on Sunday.
Oil prices remained above US$100 a barrel with no peace deal in sight and traffic in the Strait of Hormuz still limited. Negotiations between the US and Iran continued over the weekend with the countries assessing responses from each other.
"Peace talks have been stalled as both sides refuse to move on their respective red lines," ANZ analysts said in a note.
Trump has made a nuclear deal with Tehran a priority, while Iran has proposed to set aside nuclear issues until after the war ends and the foes agree to lift opposing blockades on Gulf shipping.
On Sunday, the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and their allies, or Opec+, said they will raise oil output targets by 188,000 barrels per day in June for seven members, the third consecutive monthly rise.
The increase is the same as that agreed for May minus the share of the United Arab Emirates, which left Opec on May 1. However, the higher volume will remain largely on paper as long as the Iran war continues to disrupt Gulf oil supplies through the Strait of Hormuz.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Any US 'interference' in Hormuz would be ceasefire violation: Iran
Article:
TEHERAN: A senior Iranian official warned on Monday that Teheran would consider any US attempt to interfere in the Strait of Hormuz a breach of the ceasefire.
"Any American interference in the new maritime regime of the Strait of Hormuz will be considered a violation of the ceasefire," Ebrahim Azizi, head of the national security commission in Iran's parliament, posted on X.
The comment came after President Donald Trump announced a plan for US forces to escort ships through the blocked Strait of Hormuz beginning Monday.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia leads demand for sustainable construction, survey shows
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysians are showing stronger demand for sustainable construction than the industry itself, according to the 2026 Sustainable Construction Barometer by Saint-Gobain's Sustainable Construction Observatory.
The study, which for the first time includes Malaysia, surveyed 30,000 citizens and 4,800 construction-sector stakeholders across 30 countries, including government representatives, professionals, students and industry associations.
It found a clear gap in priorities, with 71 per cent of Malaysians saying sustainable construction should be a priority, above the global average of 63 per cent.
However, only 55 per cent of local industry stakeholders agreed, lagging both domestic public sentiment and the global stakeholder average of 65 per cent.
Despite this divide, more than 60 per cent of Malaysian stakeholders believe sustainable construction can deliver equal or greater value than conventional building methods, particularly in terms of economic, environmental, social and heritage outcomes.
The report suggests both citizens and industry players see stronger awareness and cross-sector collaboration as key to closing the gap.
Architects and building engineers were identified as the most influential groups in driving progress.
Saint-Gobain Malaysia and Singapore chief executive officer Lynette Siow said that in Malaysia's rapidly urbanising landscape, climate resilience, quality housing, and inclusive growth are becoming increasingly interconnected, making sustainability a rising priority for its citizens.
"Public expectations can become a transforming force to encourage clearer policies, reinforce the need for stronger alignment and increase collaboration across the ecosystem.
"Strengthening awareness and shared understanding will be key to translating intent into action, spurring developers, contractors, policymakers, financiers, and manufacturers to find solutions that balance environmental ambition with social and economic realities."
she said in a statement.
Meanwhile, the findings also highlighted limited public understanding, with only 29 per cent of Malaysians saying they are familiar with sustainable construction.
Among those who are aware, key definitions include climate-resilient buildings (35 per cent), adaptable designs (34 per cent), and healthier living environments (32 per cent).
While industry awareness remains high at 87 per cent, the study noted that 13 per cent of stakeholders are still unfamiliar with the concept.
Both citizens and stakeholders identified raising awareness and improving collaboration as key drivers to accelerate adoption, with around 40 per cent of citizens and 45 per cent of stakeholders highlighting this need.
In this regard, Siow said sustainable construction in Malaysia is increasingly being viewed through its everyday impact.
"Guided by our purpose of 'Making the World a Better Home', we are committed to supporting the industry in the country and around the world by enabling smarter material choices, fostering collective responsibility, and delivering solutions that build not only greener buildings but also stronger, more resilient communities for generations to come," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: JS-SEZ buzz lifts Johor property stocks ahead of state polls
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Fresh news flow on the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) has stirred renewed interest in the property sector, particularly among Johor-centric property names, as the Johor state elections draw nearer, according to CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd.
The firm said trading activity in the property sector surged, with the FBM KLCI Property Index rising 2.8 per cent at the close on April 28, 2026, bringing its year-to-date gain to 14.8 per cent.
It added that the JS-SEZ investment blueprint and masterplan are currently undergoing review by Malaysia's National Investment Council before being tabled to the Malaysian cabinet for final approval.
Initially slated for launch on March 30, 2026, the JS-SEZ is now set to be rolled out by the first half of 2026 (1H26) following further refinements to its content.
"While the JS-SEZ is still a work in progress, we opine that the crystallisation of a
A formalised agreement marks another significant step that strengthens cross-border economic integration between Malaysia and Singapore.
"Broadly, the JS-SEZ is projected to generate RM260 billion in gross domestic product (GDP) for Johor by 2030, and we expect this wealth creation to trickle down into the state's property sector," it said in a note.
Beyond the JS-SEZ, CIMB Securities believes that the property sector's sentiment-driven re-rating has been boosted by investor repositioning in property stocks deemed potential beneficiaries of the MY Value Up programme launched on April 20, despite the actual mechanism remaining undisclosed.
The firm highlighted that Gamuda Bhd, IJM Corporation Bhd, Sime Darby Property Bhd, SP Setia Bhd, Eco World Development Group Bhd and Sunway Bhd meet the minimum RM4 billion market capitalisation requirement, placing them among 88 companies selected for the voluntary programme.
Overall, CIMB Securities has maintained an "Overweight" stance on the property sector, with UEM Sunrise Bhd, SP Setia, Sunway, Eco World and Mah Sing Group Bhd identified as preferred JS-SEZ plays due to their sizeable Johor land banks.
It added that SP Setia stands out among value-up proxies, trading at the steepest discount to revalued net asset value at 65 per cent and a price-to-book value of 0.4 times.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's banking sector faces complex cybersecurity landscape
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's banking sector is facing an increasingly complex cybersecurity landscape, as rapid digital transformation, the rise of artificial intelligence (AI), and deeper system interconnectivity accelerate risk exposure across financial institutions.
AKATI Sekurity chief executive officer Krishna Rajagopal said the rise in cyber incidents reflects not only a higher volume of attacks but also a structural shift in the way threats are emerging.
He said Malaysia and the wider Asean region are undergoing rapid digitalisation, while machine-to-machine interactions are expanding sharply, creating a larger and more complex attack surface for financial systems.
"Usually one of these factors on its own is bad enough, but when all three come together, you don't get linear growth but a compound one, and that's the biggest problem we're facing today," he explained.
While banks have increased their investments in cybersecurity and regulatory frameworks led by Bank Negara Malaysia have become more robust, Rajagopal said defensive measures are still struggling to keep pace with the expanding attack surface.
Modern banking systems now rely on thousands of application programming interfaces (APIs), cloud-based infrastructure, and multiple fintech integrations, all of which create new entry points for attackers.
"The attack surface is growing much faster than their existing controls can keep up. This is an industry that is shipping new features every week or every month on mobile devices, integrating with payment providers and fintech partners, and running hybrid environments across on-premise and multi-cloud systems," he added.
Beyond direct financial losses, Rajagopal said cyber incidents threaten the core of what banks offer: credibility.
He argued that trust remains the most valuable asset for financial institutions, and once compromised, it is far harder to restore than any technical system.
Although the financial cost of a breach can be substantial, including regulatory penalties, legal expenses, investigations and operational disruptions, Rajagopal said the longer-term reputational damage can be even more severe.
He also pointed to persistent structural weaknesses within the industry, including poor visibility over non-human identities such as automated systems and service accounts, vulnerabilities in password recovery processes, and the unregulated use of AI tools by employees.
At the same time, he said a shortage of specialised cybersecurity talent and limited expertise at the board level continue to weaken institutional oversight.
"If cybersecurity is treated as just an IT issue, that is already a risk in itself. It is not merely a technical problem. It is a business risk, a governance issue, and ultimately a matter of board accountability," he said.
As threats continue to evolve, Rajagopal said financial institutions must shift their approach from focusing solely on prevention to building resilience.
That means preparing for breaches as an inevitability and strengthening their ability to detect, contain and recover from incidents quickly.
Without that shift, he warned, the gap between attackers and defenders will continue to widen, leaving banks increasingly exposed in a rapidly changing digital environment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From volatility to fragility: Rethinking exposure in a changed world
Article:
In a world where global shocks travel faster, deeper, and with less predictability than ever before, Malaysia stands out as a classic "high beta economy"—one that amplifies both the gains and the risks of globalisation.
Traditionally, such dynamics were understood through the lens of VUCA—a world defined by volatility, uncertainty, complexity, and ambiguity. Today, however, that framing is increasingly insufficient.
The emerging reality is better captured by BANI: a world that is not just volatile, but brittle; not just uncertain, but anxious; not just complex, but nonlinear; and at times, incomprehensible.
Within this evolving landscape, Malaysia's economic structure takes on a new meaning. Exports account for over 60 per cent of GDP, placing the country among the most externally orientated economies globally.
This has long been a source of strength. Yet in a BANI world, it also represents a point of structural sensitivity—where external shocks do not merely transmit but can cascade in unexpected ways.
High Beta in a BANI System: Amplification Meets Fragility
Malaysia's high beta characteristics were built on decades of successful integration into the global economy. Participation in global supply chains—particularly in the electrical and electronics sector—has anchored growth, enabled industrial upgrading, and positioned the country within critical nodes of international production.
In a VUCA world, this translated into manageable volatility. External shocks were disruptive, but broadly cyclical and somewhat predictable. Policy responses could be calibrated accordingly.
In a BANI world, however, the nature of shocks has changed.
Recent disruptions—ranging from supply chain breakdowns to geopolitical tensions across key trade corridors and energy chokepoints—have demonstrated that the global system is no longer merely volatile.
It is increasingly fragile and nonlinear. Small disruptions can trigger outsized consequences. Localised tensions can cascade into global dislocations.
For a high-beta economy like Malaysia, this creates a compounding effect:
• External shocks arrive faster
• Transmission mechanisms are less predictable.
• Impacts are often disproportionate
Malaysia is no longer just an amplifier of global cycles. In a BANI system, it risks becoming an amplifier of systemic fragility unless carefully managed.
Future-Proofing Through Strategic Resilience
The imperative, therefore, is not to retreat from openness but to future-proof it.
This requires a shift from efficiency-driven integration to resilience-driven integration—what may be termed strategic openness.
The goal is to remain globally connected while reducing the risks associated with overconcentration, single-point dependencies, and external demand shocks.
Future-proofing a high-beta economy in a BANI world involves several key transitions:
• From concentration to diversification Expanding export markets and supply chain partners to reduce exposure to any single geography or demand source.
• From participation to value capture: Moving up the value chain within global industries, particularly in high-technology sectors, to enhance economic complexity and resilience.
• From external dependence to domestic buffering Strengthening domestic demand, services sectors, and internal economic linkages to absorb external volatility.
• From efficiency to redundancy (where necessary): Recognising that in a BANI world, some level of redundancy—whether in supply chains, logistics, or energy security—is not inefficiency but insurance.
Equally critical is institutional strength. Sound macroeconomic management, credible fiscal frameworks, and adaptive policy institutions form the backbone of resilience.
In an environment where shocks are nonlinear and sometimes incomprehensible, policy agility becomes as important as policy discipline.
Beyond Growth: Managing Risk in an Age of Uncertainty
Ultimately, Malaysia's high beta status is not a flaw—it is a reflection of a strategic choice to engage deeply with the global economy.
That choice has delivered growth, connectivity, and opportunity. But in a world shifting from VUCA to BANI, the terms of engagement are changing.
The central challenge is no longer simply how to grow, but how to manage exposure in a system where risks are increasingly systemic, interconnected, and difficult to predict.
Future-proofing, therefore, is not about eliminating volatility—that is neither possible nor desirable. It is about managing amplitude: capturing the upside of global expansion while building sufficient buffers to withstand downside shocks.
In this context, Malaysia's task is clear. It must evolve from being a high-beta economy that reacts to global forces into one that navigates them with strategic clarity, institutional strength, and calibrated resilience.
A high beta economy in a VUCA world could succeed through adaptation. A high beta economy in a BANI world must succeed through anticipation.
That is the essence of future-proofing in an uncertain age.
===============================
Samirul Ariff Othman is an analyst of global politics, business and economics. He is an adjunct lecturer at Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS (UTP) and a senior consultant with Global Asia Consulting. He writes on global perspectives, strategy and statecraft, offering strategic insights for a complex world. The views expressed here are entirely his own.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elevated tanker rates boost shipping profits amid tight capacity
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Elevated tanker and freight rates globally are reinforcing profitability across the shipping sector, turning vessel capacity into strong earnings generators amid continued supply constraints, industry players said.
Orkim Bhd executive director and chief executive officer Captain Cheah Sin Bi said the current rate environment is supporting earnings strength across tanker operations, while disciplined chartering structures remain key to managing volatility.
He said the company mitigates market swings through a diversified chartering strategy spanning time charters, consecutive voyage charters, contracts of affreightment (COA) and spot voyages. This structure provides both earnings visibility and the flexibility to capture upside during market upturns.
Cheah said the mixed portfolio of charter contracts shields Orkim from extreme market volatility while allowing the company to benefit from stronger pricing conditions.
While cost pressures, particularly fuel and insurance, remain elevated, he noted that contractual safeguards such as bunker adjustment clauses enable the company to pass through fluctuations in global oil prices.
Under time charter arrangements, charterers bear 100 per cent of bunker and port costs, while CVC and COA structures incorporate periodic rate adjustments tied to fuel price movements, providing strong cost visibility and margin protection.
Similarly, MTT Shipping and Logistics Bhd managing director Ooi Lean Hin said geopolitical tensions around key chokepoints, including the Strait of Hormuz, have increased fuel costs but also strengthened the case for structured surcharge mechanisms across the industry.
He said rising fuel costs are mitigated through bunker adjustment factors (BAF), allowing the company to pass on these operational expense increases to customers, similar to emergency bunker surcharges introduced by major global liners.
Ooi added that MTT Shipping's focus on domestic and intra-regional routes provides a more stable demand base, while its integrated business model enables optimisation of vessel utilisation across both liner and chartering segments.
"We leverage our bespoke vessels and specialised designs to carve out niche markets for ourselves, where we have the competitive advantage to expand our market share," he said.
"In addition, our young and fuel-efficient fleet supports cost efficiency, and our flexible deployment strategy allows us to respond effectively to changing market conditions.
Ooi noted that these capabilities also position the company to future-proof its business as stricter regulations are imposed by the International Maritime Organization (IMO), such as the implementation of the Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI) and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) in January 2023.
Despite near-term volatility, Ooi said the company remains well-positioned to navigate the current environment and sustain its performance.
He said the cost of bunker fuel is one of the major components of the company's direct costs for its container shipping operations and fluctuates according to the prevailing global oil prices.
"We manage such fluctuations by factoring in a bunker adjustment factor in our freight rates, in part as recovery costs to us for the fluctuation of bunker fuel costs, enabling us to pass on increases in bunker fuel cost to our customers," he said.
Ooi said the extent to which the company can pass on higher costs ultimately depends on market conditions and the nature of the contractual arrangements.
"Our focus on domestic and intra-regional routes, where reliability, frequency, and service consistency are critical, supports our ability to maintain pricing discipline and negotiate sustainable rate structures with customers," Ooi said.
"More importantly, given our longstanding involvement in the business, we have established ourselves as Malaysia's leading container liner operator with a relatively young and fuel-efficient fleet that provides us with a structural cost advantage, helping us to achieve cost leadership and protect margins," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Middle East conflict triggers risk of buying houses under construction
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Homebuyers face mounting risks as global instability threatens to push construction material costs up by between 30 per cent and 40 per cent, exposing structural weaknesses in the housing ecosystem, according to Khazanah Research Institute (KRI).
KRI researcher Muhammad Nazhan Kamaruzuki said escalating geopolitical tensions, including conflicts in the Middle East, are expected to intensify cost pressures across the local construction sector.
While rising costs are often seen as a challenge for developers and contractors, in reality, homebuyers bear the greatest risk in such situations, he said.
"In reality, homebuyers carry the biggest risk, especially those buying homes that are still under construction. This issue is not just about construction risk but a systemic failure within the housing ecosystem," he said in a recent KRI report.
He noted that global uncertainty is already feeding through to project delays, rising development costs and uncertainty over delivery timelines, particularly under Malaysia's progressive payment model for off-plan properties.
"When cost shocks or supply disruptions occur, buyers are in a weak position because they have already made financial commitments without strong guarantees that the project will be completed," he said.
Market analysts echoed these concerns, warning that sustained cost inflation is likely to compress developers' margins and heighten execution risks across the property sector.
They said persistent volatility in key construction inputs like steel, cement and energy could force developers to either absorb higher costs or delay projects, with both scenarios ultimately affecting buyers.
Contractors, they added, are also likely to face tighter margins and potential cash flow strain, especially for fixed-price contracts signed before the recent surge in input costs.
Muhammad Nazhan stressed the need to reassess existing policy and regulatory frameworks to ensure comprehensive protection for homebuyers.
He called for stronger project monitoring mechanisms, greater developer accountability, and improvements to sale and purchase agreements, alongside a review of financing structures and project implementation to reduce risk transfer to consumers.
"Systemic reforms are needed to ensure the housing ecosystem is more resilient in facing global uncertainties, while also protecting buyers' interests," he said.
He warned that without comprehensive improvements, any future economic or geopolitical shocks could continue to negatively impact the domestic housing market.
"Homebuyers are always exposed to construction risks and financial burdens when purchasing housing projects that are still under construction.
"These risks will become more pronounced as the construction sector is expected to face rising material costs amid global instability, which could slow the national economy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia emerges as strategic shipping safe haven as global freight rates surge
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Longer shipping routes, firmer freight rates and shifting sourcing patterns are strengthening Malaysia's position as a regional logistics and energy transportation hub, particularly for tanker operators serving domestic and intra-Asia demand.
As global shipping lanes grow more fragmented, Malaysia is seen as a stable and strategically located maritime hub, offering operators a defensive base amid persistent geopolitical volatility.
Industry players said the sector is riding a structural shift in global trade flows as geopolitical tensions and supply chain disruptions underscore the country's strategic position along the Strait of Malacca, the world's second-busiest maritime chokepoint.
"We believe the near-term outlook remains strong, driven by positive underlying tanker supply and demand fundamentals," Captain Cheah Sin Bi, non-independent executive director and chief executive officer of Orkim Bhd, said.
He said a key catalyst has been escalating tensions in the Middle East, particularly around the Strait of Hormuz, which have triggered a surge in crude tanker earnings. Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) rates climbed above US$200,000 per day on certain routes in early 2026, as shipowners rerouted vessels to avoid high-risk zones.
These diversions are lengthening voyage distances, tightening effective fleet supply and pushing freight rates higher, developments that have historically favoured tanker operators.
Cheah said geopolitical disruptions are creating inefficiencies in global trade, increasing tonne-miles and reshaping regional trade patterns in ways that benefit established hubs while accelerating a shift towards alternative sourcing.
Despite heightened volatility, the tanker segment remains structurally resilient, particularly for operations like Orkim that transport essential energy products.
"Our business is driven primarily by domestic consumption, which has demonstrated strong growth, and is therefore less exposed to upstream oil price volatility or global trade disruptions. As long as energy demand remains intact, the underlying fundamentals of our segment remain strong," Cheah told Business Times.
Cheah said that while turmoil in the Middle East has raised concerns over feedstock shortages, he noted that oil flows have remained largely uninterrupted, supported by regional production and inventory buffers.
The company has also secured six contracts with key clients in the past three months, incorporating escalation clauses to offset rising costs.
"These adjustments are consistent with prevailing inflationary trends and reflect our ability to maintain pricing discipline within a stable demand environment," he said.
Globally, however, tanker operators are navigating a more complex operating environment. Vessel rerouting, higher war-risk premiums and rising bunker fuel costs are driving up overall shipping expenses, even as they support firmer freight rates.
Cheah said Orkim plays a vital role in this period by providing a secure means to move energy independent of volatile maritime routes, minimising the impact of geopolitical conflicts, and ensuring the nation's energy security.
The segment's resilience is underpinned by structurally inelastic demand for energy products. Orkim's focus on refined petroleum products such as petrol, diesel and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) further insulates it from upstream price volatility due to its domestic-orientated exposure.
Cheah said although geopolitical events continue to shape the oil trade flows in 2026, the underlying tanker demand and supply fundamentals remain supportive.
"We operate in the daily necessities segment, transporting essential energy products such as petrol, diesel, and LPG, which support national energy security. Energy, particularly oil demand, is structurally inelastic. Our fleet is fully contracted and utilised.
"Globally, oil tankers are forced to take longer routes, avoiding high-conflict areas, causing significant disruptions to global supply chains by stretching vessel availability and escalating transportation costs. Our focus on the domestic market and specific regional routes stems from lower risk exposure," he said.
Shipping Industry Remains Resilient Amid Global Disruptions, Says MTT Shipping
The global shipping industry has demonstrated structural resilience through successive shocks, from the Covid-19 pandemic to ongoing geopolitical tensions, underscoring its position as a critical enabler of global trade, MTT Shipping and Logistics Bhd managing director Ooi Lean Hin said.
According to him, the pandemic marked an unprecedented disruption to global logistics but ultimately reinforced the sector's strength, as constrained capacity and supply chain bottlenecks supported freight rates and earnings across the industry.
"The Iran war is isolated to the Gulf region. Although the effects of the oil crisis are felt globally, the disruption in shipping capacity is on a relatively smaller scale than Covid but nevertheless still positive for shipping," he told Business Times.
Ooi said from the company's perspective, MTT Shipping is not directly affected given that it mainly focuses on domestic (Peninsular Malaysia to East Malaysia and Brunei) and intra-regional routes (Indonesia, Thailand, Singapore, India and China).
"We also do not have any ships that sail through the Straits of Hormuz or Persian Gulf," he said.
He added that given the recent global trade disruptions, particularly in the Middle East, this has led to trade route diversions as well as intermediate port congestion due to staging of Gulf containers.
Before the war, the major east-west operators were already planning their return to the Suez Canal for east-west trades. They have since made a u-turn on this plan, reverting back to transits via the Cape of Good Hope."
This rerouting, combined with vessel congestion in the Gulf, has tightened global shipping capacity and supported firmer charter and freight rates, he said.
"Leveraging the growth of East Malaysia, supported by ongoing infrastructure developments and increasing economic activities, this would essentially fuel the strengthening of westbound cargo flows from East Malaysia to Peninsular Malaysia, thereby improving the overall trade balance, enhancing our vessel utilisation and driving more efficient deployment across our logistical networks."
In terms of demand, Ooi said the broader picture remains constructive. Intra-Asia and intra-Southeast Asia trade flows continue to demonstrate resilience, supported by ongoing manufacturing activity, supply chain regionalisation, and stable end-consumption within the region.
"For us, our strategic focus on domestic and intra-regional shipping routes provides a more defensive earnings profile, as we are less directly exposed to disruptions affecting long-haul East-West trade lanes. At the same time, we are able to benefit indirectly from tighter global vessel supply, which can support vessel charter rates.
"We are also observing some rebalancing of trade flows, as cargo owners prioritise reliability and shorter supply chains, which could further strengthen regional shipping demand over time," he said.
He said the relocation of production to Southeast Asia under the China+1 strategy is creating additional opportunities, which the company is well positioned to capture through its asset-backed model and regional focus, enabling it to navigate shifting trade flows and benefit from evolving market dynamics.
"We continue to monitor geopolitical developments and maintain a highly flexible operating approach to respond to any escalation in a timely manner. From an operational standpoint, we are able to adjust sailing routes, optimise schedules, and reroute vessels where necessary, ensuring service continuity while minimising disruption risks.
"Our focus on domestic and intra-regional routes provides a natural buffer, as we are not exposed to key conflict-affected corridors such as major Middle East transit routes. This enables us to sustain a relatively stable operational base, even amid heightened global uncertainty," he said.
On the risk management front, Ooi said the company is closely monitoring cost variables such as fuel prices and insurance premiums, and measures are being implemented to protect margins.
"We also maintain a prudent balance sheet and sufficient liquidity, which provides us with the financial flexibility to absorb short-term volatility and continue executing our strategy without disruption. Gearing is at 0.5, and we expect to maintain this level," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gold buying holds steady as price dips trigger renewed investor demand
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Consumer demand for gold remains steady despite ongoing price volatility, with recent price pullbacks triggering renewed buying interest and reinforcing the precious metal's role as both a store of value and a retail investment asset.
Habib Jewels Sdn Bhd group executive chairman Meer Habib said buying patterns have evolved significantly in recent years, with a growing segment of consumers, particularly men, shifting towards gold as an investment rather than purely for personal adornment.
"Previously, most purchases were for jewellery. Now, we see more customers buying gold bars as an investment," he told reporters after a dialogue session themed 'Gold in an Uncertain World' today.
Despite this structural change, he said that jewellery demand remains firm, supported by cultural gifting traditions and its dual role as both wearable luxury and an investment asset.
"For customers who felt they missed the earlier rally, the recent decline is seen as an opportunity. We are seeing increased buying interest.
"Jewellery carries a dual appeal. It can be worn and also serves as an investment. That continues to attract customers, especially women," he added.
Meer Habib said that trading activity has also picked up with more customers selling or exchanging older pieces for new designs, supporting overall sales momentum.
On the broader market outlook, he said gold prices have experienced short-term volatility amid geopolitical tensions, particularly in the Middle East, and disruptions to key oil supply routes such as the Strait of Hormuz.
"Overall, gold prices have been on an upward trend. Two years ago, prices rose about 65 per cent, followed by another 27 per cent increase last year," he said.
He added that recent price softness, where gold hovered below RM600 per gramme compared with earlier highs of RM650 to RM700, was largely driven by temporary factors, including countries reallocating reserves to secure energy supplies.
"Normally, during war, gold prices rise because it is seen as a safe haven. This time, some countries had to sell gold to buy oil, which led to a slight pullback," he said.
Meer Habib stressed that such movements are short-term in nature, while the longer-term outlook remains supported by strong fundamentals.
"Many central banks are now increasing their gold reserves instead of holding US dollars, especially after what happened during the Russia-Ukraine sanctions.
"That has further strengthened gold's position as a reserve asset," added Meer Habib.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rubio to visit Rome, meet Pope Leo after Trump row
Article:
ROME: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio will meet Pope Leo on a trip to Rome this week, in the wake of the pontiff's clash with President Donald Trump, a Vatican source said Sunday.
The source confirmed Italian media reports about the meeting, which newspapers said would take place on Thursday in an attempt to "thaw" relations.
It comes just weeks after Trump's extraordinary criticism of Pope Leo XIV over the Catholic leader's anti-war rhetoric.
An Italian government source earlier told AFP that Rubio would also meet Vatican Secretary of State Pietro Parolin and Italian Foreign Minister Antonio Tajani.
Rubio had asked for a meeting with Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, one of Trump's closest European allies, whom he turned on after she defended the pope, the source added.
Media reports said he was also due to meet Defence Minister Guido Crosetto, amid a deepening rift in transatlantic ties over the Middle East war.
- TRUMP'S 'UNACCEPTABLE' REMARKS -
Leo, 70, will on Friday mark one year as leader of the world's 1.4 billion Catholics, after being elected by cardinals on May 8, 2025, following the death of Pope Francis.
As the first ever pope from the United States, his words have arguably carried more weight in Washington than previous pontiffs – and he has used them, criticising notably the Trump administration's crackdown on immigration.
But it was the pontiff's increasing anti-war rhetoric, particularly following the US-Israeli attacks on Iran, that triggered Trump's ire.
Leo declared Trump's threat to destroy Iran "unacceptable" and urged Americans to demand that US lawmakers "work for peace."
The US president slammed the pontiff in a social media post as "WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy."
Trump also said he was "not a big fan of Pope Leo" and that he does not "want a pope who thinks it's OK for Iran to have a nuclear weapon."
The pontiff responded by saying he had a "moral duty to speak out" against war – and then sparked more headlines with a speech in Cameroon lambasting "tyrants" ransacking the world.
However, he insisted afterwards that the remarks were written long before the row, and said he had not intended to start a new debate with the US president.
- TRUMP'S TROOPS THREAT -
Christians across the world expressed their solidarity with the pope, and Meloni condemned Trump's remarks as "unacceptable" – prompting the president to turn his fire on her.
"I'm shocked at her. I thought she had courage, but I was wrong," the US president said in an interview with Italian daily Corriere della Sera.
He accused Meloni – a far-right leader who has sought to act as a bridge between diverging US and European views – of failing to help the United States with NATO.
Trump has threatened to pull US troops from Italy, saying Rome "has not been of any help to us" in the Iran war.
He has made a similar threat towards Spain, while the Pentagon has announced it will withdraw 5,000 US troops from Germany.
As of Dec 31, 2025, there were 12,662 active-duty US troops in Italy and 3,814 in Spain. In Germany, there were 36,436.
In Sunday's address in St Peter's Square, the pope marked World Press Freedom Day. "Unfortunately, this right is often violated - sometimes blatantly, sometimes in more subtle ways," he said.
"Let us remember the many journalists and reporters who have fallen victim to wars and violence."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EAIC flags gaps in fuel subsidy governance amid concerns over leakages, smuggling
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Enforcement Agency Integrity Commission (EAIC) has flagged several high-risk governance lapses that could arise in managing an energy supply crisis, particularly in the distribution of aid and subsidised fuel.
Its chairman, Tan Sri Dr Ismail Bakar, said the risks were heightened when carrying out enforcement under urgent conditions to curb leakages of public resources, including subsidies for RON95 petrol, diesel and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG).
Based on its oversight of 20 enforcement agencies, EAIC identified key risk areas during crises, including cross-border smuggling of subsidised fuel — especially at entry points where officers may abuse their authority or fail to comply with the standard operating procedure (SOP).
"For instance, enforcement officers at border points may allow vehicles carrying subsidised fuel to pass without thorough checks, resulting in leakages," he said.
The commission also highlighted the risk of misappropriation of operational fuel supplies among enforcement personnel, noting that such allocations could be diverted for personal use if controls were weak.
Ismail said EAIC had previously received complaints and conducted investigations into such cases, underscoring the need for stricter internal controls.
He stressed that effective monitoring at petrol stations and other high-risk locations remained critical, including preventing  misuse of subsidies through the use of identity cards by ineligible parties.
Commenting on the government's move to deploy police personnel to 151 high-risk petrol stations, Ismail described it as a proactive step to strengthen enforcement and reduce leakages while allowing  Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry officers to focus on other hotspots, such as fishermen's diesel depots, illegal jetties and bunkering activities.
However, he said the effectiveness of these measures remained at an early stage and required continuous monitoring.
He added that EAIC had yet to detect clear signs of abuse of power following the intervention, but would continue to track complaints closely.
Ismail also called for stronger inter-agency coordination through structured mechanisms, including setting up a centralised crisis command centre, clearer SOP and improved information-sharing systems to enhance efficiency and reduce overlaps.
Meanwhile, the General Operations Force (GOF) has given  assurance that there has been no misuse of fuel quotas among its personnel deployed along the Malaysia-Thailand border.
GOF Southeast Brigade commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain said fuel usage among more than 800 personnel in Kelantan was being monitored, with no irregularities detected to date.
"We remain fully supportive of government policies and adhere to all directives related to cost-saving measures, including fuel usage and operational expenditure," he said.
Ahmad Radzi also described the government's procurement of electric bikes for GOF as a forward-looking measure to reduce fuel dependency and enhance operational efficiency.
Echoing this, GOF Northern Brigade commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Balveer Singh said there had been no reports of misuse within his brigade, with monitoring teams carrying out weekly inspections.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran tells US: Choose 'impossible war' or accept deal as peace talks stall
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's Revolutionary Guards on Sunday said the United States faced a choice between an "impossible" military operation or a deal with Teheran, after President Donald Trump disparaged Iran's latest peace proposal.
Negotiations between the two countries have been deadlocked since a ceasefire came into effect on April 8, with only one round of direct peace talks held so far.
Iran's foreign ministry said Teheran had submitted a 14-point plan "focused on ending the war" and that Washington had already responded to it in a message to Pakistani mediators.
"We are reviewing this and will take whatever response is necessary regarding it," spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei told state television.
Trump, however, had already dismissed the offer.
"I will soon be reviewing the plan that Iran has just sent to us, but can't imagine that it would be acceptable in that they have not yet paid a big enough price for what they have done to Humanity, and the World, over the last 47 years," Trump said, in a post on his Truth Social platform.
US news website Axios reported, citing two sources briefed on the proposal, that it set "a one-month deadline for negotiations on a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, end the US naval blockade and permanently end the war in Iran and in Lebanon."
In a statement on Sunday, the Revolutionary Guards sought to put the onus back on Trump, saying he must choose between "an impossible operation or a bad deal with the Islamic Republic of Iran."
"The room for US decision-making has narrowed," they said.
Washington's European allies are concerned that the longer the strait remains closed the more their economies will suffer, and German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul demanded it be reopened.
In a call with his Iranian counterpart Abbas Araghchi, Wadephul stressed that Germany supported a negotiated solution but that "Iran must completely and verifiably renounce nuclear weapons and immediately open the Strait of Hormuz."
- 'IF THEY MISBEHAVE' -
In a brief interview with reporters in West Palm Beach, Florida, on Saturday, the US president declined to specify what could trigger new American military action.
"If they misbehave, if they do something bad, but right now, we'll see," he said. "But it's a possibility that could happen, certainly."
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US naval blockade was only part of a broader economic embargo.
"We are suffocating the regime, and they are not able to pay their soldiers. This is a real economic blockade, and it is in all parts of government – all hands on deck," he said, in a Fox News interview.
In yet more bellicose rhetoric, Mohsen Rezaei, a military adviser to Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, said Iranian forces would sink US ships.
"The US is the only pirate in the world that possesses aircraft carriers. Our ability to confront pirates is no less than our ability to sink warships. Prepare to face a graveyard of your carriers and forces," he posted on X.
There is no evidence that Iran has sunk any US military vessels during the war.
Axios reported earlier in the week that Trump's envoy Steve Witkoff had asked for Teheran's nuclear programme to be put back on the negotiating table.
But Iran's mission to the UN pointed to the massive US nuclear arsenal, accusing Washington on Saturday of "hypocritical behaviour" towards Iran's own atomic ambitions.
- 'TRYING TO ENDURE' -
Iran has maintained a stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz since the war began, choking off major flows of oil, gas and fertiliser to the world economy, while the United States has imposed a counter-blockade on Iranian ports.
Oil prices are about 50 per cent above pre-war levels.
The deputy speaker of Iran's parliament, Ali Nikzad, said that under draft legislation being considered for managing the strait, 30 per cent of tolls collected would go towards military infrastructure, with the rest earmarked for "economic development."
"Managing the Strait of Hormuz is more important than acquiring nuclear weapons," he said.
In Iran, the war's economic toll is deepening, with oil exports curtailed and inflation surging past 50 percent.
"Everyone is trying to endure it, but... they are falling apart," 40-year-old Amir, a Teheran resident, told an AFP reporter based outside the country.
"We still have not seen much of the economic effects because everyone had a bit of savings. They had some gold and dollars for a rainy day. When they run out, things will change."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Despite tensions, Germany's Merz insists on continuing cooperation with Trump
Article:
BERLIN: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said Sunday he would not give up on working with US President Donald Trump, despite a spat between the leaders over the war in Iran.
"I am not giving up on working on the transatlantic relationship," Merz told public broadcaster ARD in an interview set to air Sunday night. "Nor am I giving up on working with Donald Trump."
Merz sought to downplay any tensions with Trump, arguing for instance that an abrupt public announcement that the US would pull 5,000 troops from bases in Germany was not surprising and should not be seen as retaliation.
"It may be being exaggerated a bit, but it's not new," Merz told talk show host Caren Miosga.
Criticism of the US-Israeli war against Iran from Merz and other European leaders has clearly aggravated Trump. The latest rift came after Merz on April 27 said that Iran was "humiliating" Washington at the negotiating table.
That provoked a series of angry responses from Washington, including Trump's comments that Merz was doing a "terrible" job as chancellor.
Merz on Sunday said the US decision on troops had "no connection" with the recent disagreements.
- 'IMPORTANT PARTNERS' -
The chancellor also appeared to confirm that a planned deployment of US long-range Tomahawk cruise missiles to Germany – announced by former US president Joe Biden – was being called off, at least for the time being.
He cited depleted arsenals because of the wars in Iran and Ukraine as the reason.
"The Americans themselves don't have enough at the moment," he said.
"Objectively speaking, there is hardly any possibility from the US of relinquishing weapons systems of this kind."
But he insisted the "train has not departed" for possible cooperation in the future, and that European Nato powers could continue deterring Russia without those particular missiles or US forces.
Trump's criticism of European leaders has not been confined to Merz.
He previously ridiculed British Prime Minister Kier Starmer, and on Thursday told reporters that "Italy has not been of any help to us and Spain has been horrible, absolutely horrible."
But Merz insisted that he still shared Trump's goal of ensuring Iran never obtains an atomic weapon, even if he has criticised the US handling of the conflict.
"We have a different view of this war. That's no secret," Merz said in the interview. "I'm not the only one who feels that way."
Merz insisted that Trump respects his right to hold differing views – though perhaps "a little less for the moment."
"But that does not alter the fact that I remain convinced that the Americans are important partners for us – our most important partners in the North Atlantic alliance," Merz said.--AFp
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kedah rice bowl stalls as drought delays planting [WATCH]
Article:
ALOR STAR: In the heart of Kedah's rice bowl, a familiar landscape of green padi field has fallen eerily still. Almost 90 per cent of rice farmers, including those in the Muda Agricultural Development Authority (Mada) areas, have yet to begin planting for Season 1/2026 — a delay now stretching across weeks and threatening to unsettle the country's staple food supply chain.
For farmers gathered quietly at Kampung Alor Setoi, the uncertainty is no longer abstract. It is measured in dry fields, rising costs, and dwindling water reserves.
Pertubuhan Persaudaraan Pesawah Malaysia (PeSAWAH) chairman Abdul Rashid Yob said the situation had reached a critical point, warning that the disruption could have long-term implications on Malaysia's rice self-sufficiency.
"The primary issue is the low dam water levels. The Muda Dam is still at a critical stage, and priority is being given to domestic use rather than irrigation for padi fields," he said.
"Even if water is released soon, many farmers still cannot plant because the costs have become too high to bear."
Under the original Mada schedule, planting was supposed to have begun in phases from April 15, followed by April 29 and May 13. All three phases have now been missed or postponed.
Abdul Rashid painted a stark picture of the financial strain on farmers already grappling with volatile input costs.
"Machinery costs have skyrocketed. Tractor rental has increased by up to 66 per cent, while harvesters are up around 38 per cent. Overall, operational costs have surged by more than 50 per cent," he said.
"This does not even include fertilisers and other inputs. Padi prices remain stagnant, so farmers simply cannot absorb these increases."
His voice hardened as he described what he called an unsustainable imbalance in the system.
"If padi prices go up, rice prices must be adjusted, or the government must step in to stabilise the situation," he said.
Even recent rainfall, he added, had done little to change ground conditions.
"We need significantly more rain to replenish the canals. At the moment, conditions are still not suitable for planting, even though we are already in May," he said.
Abdul Rashid urged the government to act swiftly, framing the issue as one of national importance rather than sectoral concern.
"Just as we protect national sovereignty, we must protect food sovereignty. Rice is our staple food, and farmers are the frontliners ensuring its supply," he said.
Official data underscores the severity of the crisis. Combined storage across Mada's three dams stands at 38.36 per cent. The Pedu Dam is at 39.36 per cent, Ahning at 51.31 per cent, but the Muda Dam — the system's lifeline — remains critically low at 8.24 per cent.
The current predicament traces back to March, when Mada announced a suspension of irrigation supply due to prolonged hot and dry weather. Farmers were instructed to switch to dry direct seeding — a method previously used in 2017 and 2020, but one that typically yields lower returns.
PeSAWAH has strongly opposed the approach. Abdul Rashid warned that yields could drop by as much as 10 per cent, with farmers bearing the full burden of losses.
"Only two in 10 farmers will achieve a successful harvest under dry seeding. The rest face pests, weeds and poor nutrient uptake," he said.
Mada chairman Datuk Dr Ismail Salleh, however, defended the agency's position, saying the dry spell had left little alternative.
"This has been a prolonged drought. Our main water source is the three dams, and levels are simply too low," he said.
"The current combined capacity is about 38 per cent. We need at least 45 per cent before we can release water for padi fields."
He confirmed that the first two planting phases — April 15 and April 29 — had already been missed, while the May 13 phase will also not proceed.
"We will not be planting in the coming phase," he said.
Ismail said Mada was hoping for sustained rainfall in the coming weeks to replenish dam levels and had engaged the Meteorological Department on cloud seeding, though previous attempts yielded mixed results.
As an interim measure, he suggested that farmers located near irrigation channels could use pumps to access limited water supplies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three die on Atlantic cruise ship from suspected hantavirus: WHO
Article:
PRAIA, Cape Verde: Three people have died on a cruise ship in the Atlantic, the WHO said Sunday, one a confirmed case of hantavirus – an illness usually transmitted to humans from rodents.
The outbreak occurred on the MV Hondius, travelling from Ushuaia in Argentina to Cape Verde.
"To date, one case of hantavirus infection has been laboratory confirmed, and there are five additional suspected cases," the WHO told AFP.
"Of the six affected individuals, three have died and one is currently in intensive care in South Africa."
Earlier Sunday, South Africa's health ministry told AFP there had been an outbreak of a "severe acute respiratory illness", which had killed at least two people, with a third in intensive care in Johannesburg.
The patient treated in Johannesburg tested positive for a hantavirus, a family of viruses that can cause hemorrhagic fever, South African spokesperson Foster Mohale said.
In its statement, the WHO said: "Hantavirus infections are typically linked to environmental exposure (exposure to infected rodents' urine or faeces).
"While rare, hantavirus may spread between people, and can lead to severe respiratory illness and requires careful patient monitoring, support and response."
- HUSBAND AND WIFE -
The first person to develop symptoms was a 70-year-old passenger. He died on board the ship and his body was currently on the island of Saint Helena, a British territory in the South Atlantic, Mohale the South African spokesman said.
His 69-year-old wife also fell ill on board and was evacuated to South Africa, where she died in a Johannesburg hospital, he said, adding that they were not yet able to confirm the victims' nationalities.
The third case, a 69-year-old Briton, was also evacuated to Johannesburg, where he was being treated in intensive care.
A source close to the case speaking on condition of anonymity had said a Dutch couple were among the dead. The third fatality was still on board the ship.
Discussions were under way to decide whether two other sick passengers should be placed in isolation in hospital in Cape Verde, after which the ship would continue to Spain's Canary Islands, the anonymous source said.
The WHO said it was "facilitating coordination" between national authorities and the ship's operators to organise the medical evacuation of two passengers with symptoms.
The MV Hondius is listed as a polar cruise ship on the websites of several travel agencies. It is operated by a Dutch-based tour company, Oceanwide Expeditions.
One of the cruises offers an itinerary departing from Ushuaia for Cape Verde, with stops in the islands of South Georgia and Saint Helena.
According to several online ship-tracking sites, the MV Hondius was just off the port of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde, on Sunday.
The vessel can accommodate around 170 passengers and has some 70 crew members.
Humans can catch hantaviruses from contact with infected mice or rats or their droppings, or being bitten or inhaling contaminated dust. There are multiple types of hantaviruses in different parts of the world, with different symptoms.
AFP contacted the cruise operator Oceanwide Expeditions but has not yet had a reply.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Massive search continues for two missing US soldiers in Morocco
Article:
RABAT: Two US soldiers that disappeared while on a training deployment in southern Morocco were last seen near seaside cliffs and may have fallen into the ocean, a US defence official confirmed to AFP.
A massive land, air and sea search was launched by US, Moroccan and allied forces in Cap Draa Training Area after the service members went missing late Saturday, both militaries said.
The search remained underway as of shortly before 2000 GMT on Sunday, the US official told AFP.
"I can confirm this incident is not related to terrorism but appears to be an accident," the official said. "Initial reports indicate the two soldiers may have fallen into the ocean."
The Wall Street Journal reported that officials believe the missing pair went on a hike after training had concluded. AFP has not been able to independently confirm this.
CBS News reported that one of its journalists heard helicopters "throughout the night" and into Sunday after a "base-wide head-count" failed to account for the two soldiers.
Morocco's armed forces confirmed they were part of the search in a post on Facebook.
The US defence official said that several helicopters, vessels, drones, mountaineers and divers were involved in the search.
The troops were taking part in an annual joint military training exercise called African Lion, which brings together US, allied African, and Nato militaries for drills in northern and west Africa.
The US military describes the training as "Africa Command's largest premier, joint, annual exercise," bringing together "more than 10,000 participants from more than 20 nations, including contingents from Nato." It is hosted by Morocco, Ghana, Senegal and Tunisia.
In 2012, two US Marines were killed and two more were injured in an aircraft crash in the same area during that year's African Lion exercise.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Spanish PM's plane in emergency landing in Turkiye
Article:
ANKARA: A plane carrying Spanish Premier Pedro Sanchez made an emergency landing in Turkiye on Sunday evening following technical problems, a source told AFP, confirming media reports.
According to media reports, Sanchez was on the way to neighbouring Armenia for a meeting of the European Political Community (EPC) when the incident happened.
The source, who required anonymity, did not say what kind of problem the aircraft had encountered.
European leaders are gathering in the Armenian capital Yerevan for the EPC meeting, which starts Monday.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump calls Iran talks "very positive" as US plans to escort ships through Hormuz
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Sunday that "very positive discussions" were under way with Iran on finding a solution to the crisis, but nevertheless added that the United States will soon start escorting ships through the blocked Strait of Hormuz.
Earlier, a spokesman for Iran's foreign ministry, Esmaeil Baqaei, told state television that Teheran had submitted a 14-point plan "focused on ending the war," and that Washington had responded to it in a message to Pakistani mediators.
"I am fully aware that my Representatives are having very positive discussions with the Country of Iran, and that these discussions could lead to something very positive for all," Trump said on his Truth Social platform.
The US-Israel alliance launched attacks on Iran on Feb 28, killing the Islamic republic's supreme leader. Teheran responded with strikes on US military bases and Israeli targets in the region.
A ceasefire came into effect April 8, and there has been one round of direct peace talks in Islamabad since, with the two countries deadlocked.
Iran has maintained a stranglehold on the Strait of Hormuz since the war began, choking off major flows of oil, gas and fertiliser to the world economy, while the United States has imposed a counter-blockade on Iranian ports.
Trump said "countries from all over the world" had requested American aid in navigating through the key waterway and out of the Gulf.
"For the good of Iran, the Middle East, and the United States, we have told these Countries that we will guide their Ships safely out of these restricted Waterways, so that they can freely and ably get on with their business," Trump said.
"This process, Project Freedom, will begin Monday morning, Middle East time."
He called it a "humanitarian gesture," saying many of the marooned ships were "running low on food, and everything else necessary for largescale crews to stay on board in a healthy and sanitary manner."
As of April 29, more than 900 commercial vessels were located in the Gulf, according to maritime intelligence firm AXSMarine. There had been more than 1,100 at the start of the conflict.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Backyard experiment blossoms into agrotourism venture
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
Sunlight filters through the canopy, with grapevines supported by a structured trellis system that allows for controlled cultivation and easier maintenance.
What began as a backyard experiment has grown into a budding agrotourism venture for former graphic artist Wan Yusoff Abdul Rahman.
The 42-year-old now tends a grape orchard at his home in Kampung Belukar, Wakaf Baru, which has drawn visitors curious to see and taste locally grown grapes.
His journey into farming began in 2019, driven by a desire to try something new while spending more time with his family.
"I started with a few plants around the house. Now I have about 200 grapevines, with 60 already bearing fruit," he said, adding that the variety he grows — Krasava, originally from Ukraine — is not widely available in Malaysia.
Krasava is a high-yielding hybrid grape known for its striking, finger-shaped purple berries and early ripening.
Wan Yusoff said each productive vine yields between 12kg and 15kg of grapes per season, with current output reaching about 900kg.
"I sell the grapes at RM50 per kg and there is also demand for the plants," he said.
Wan Yusoff allows visitors to enter his orchard for free on Fridays. Among the regulars are school groups keen to learn about grape cultivation.
Wan Yusoff is often seen inspecting the vines, checking the ripeness of the grapes as he moves through the orchard.
Looking ahead, he plans to transform the orchard into a full-fledged agrotourism destination.
Wan Yusoff said he received a RM169,000 grant from an agency under the Kemubu Agricultural Development Authority earlier this year to develop facilities, including a seminar hall, dining area, public toilets and a cafe.
"Construction is expected to begin this month," he said. "The idea is to create a place where people can relax while learning about agriculture."
Future plans include a "pick-your-own grapes" experience, a wedding photo booth area and expansion of the orchard by another 0.8ha.
Wan Yusoff said the orchard currently attracts about 10,000 visitors annually and he expects the number to double once the agrotourism project is completed.
"This is more than just farming. It is about creating a space for people to enjoy nature and spend time with their families," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadhlina orders Moral Education subject review
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek has directed the Examinations Syndicate to review the Moral Education subject for the Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) following complaints from the Indian community.
"When concerns about Moral Education were raised, and given the anxieties of parents and the community, I directed the Examinations Syndicate to review and improve it," said Fadhlina at the Kalvi Yutham, an educational and motivational programme for Tamil students at SJK (T) Castlefield in Puchong yesterday.
Present were Selangor Education Department director Datin Wan Nor Ashikin Abu Kassim and Sri Murugan Centre director Surain Kanda.
Surain raised the issue ahead of Fadhlina's address, and the minister said she was grateful for the community's candour.
"I thank Surain for this. This is what we want when we talk about these issues. I cannot act as though I know everything. The era of the government knowing everything is over.
"This is the era of working together to improve education."
Surain said his organisation had raised concerns about the Moral Education subject through official channels.
"(Fadhlina) gave her assurance that the Moral Education issue would be revisited seriously."
Surain said the community's main grievances with the subject concerned the closed-book exam paper, marking system and grading threshold.
He said it should be an open system so that students would have past-year papers for revision.
Surain also called for the holistic marking method used for language subjects to be replaced with analytical marking used for other subjects.
On the grading threshold, he said students needed to score 95 or above to obtain an A+, a bar he described as unreasonably high.
Surain also said the community's request to increase matriculation slots for Indian students to 2,500 without encroaching on other quotas was modest and targeted.
In response, Fadhlina reaffirmed the government's commitment to automatically offer matriculation places to students who scored straight As in their SPM examinations, regardless of their ethnic background.
She said the concerns among the Indian community of high-achieving students being denied matriculation places were legitimate but added that the government was addressing them.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hundreds turn up in style for 'power nap contest'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
Under hazy spring sunshine on Saturday, hundreds of young Seoulites turned up at a park by the Han River to try to do something many overworked South Koreans never get enough of — sleep.
The Seoul Metropolitan Government staged its third annual spring event, dubbed a "power nap contest", from 3pm under a refreshed set of admission requirements for would-be participants: wear outfits befitting either a sleeping beauty or prince, come tired and with a full belly.
For a metropolis that famously runs on 24-hour shopping malls, competitive hustle and iced Americanos, the underlying exhaustion on the lawn was palpable.
"Between exam prep and part-time jobs, I survive on three or four hours of sleep a night, patching it up with desk naps during the day," said Park Jun-seok, who showed up draped in the silken robes of a Joseon Dynasty monarch.
"I'm here to show off my napping skills and to demonstrate exactly how a king sleeps," said the 20-year-old university student.
Nearby, Yoo Mi-yeon, 24, an English teacher from Ilsan, north of Seoul, stood out in a plush, oversized koala-themed onesie.
"Koalas are famous for their deep slumber. I came dressed as one hoping to borrow a little of their magic," she said.
Now in its third consecutive year, the sleep competition underscores a chronic issue for South Korea.
Data show it is one of the most overworked and sleep-deprived nations among Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development members, and its people have some of the fewest sleep hours.
